archetypes.name: The Waterboy
archetypes.shortDesc: You mostly experience basketball through friends, memes, and unfortunate proximity.
archetypes.fullDesc: Basketball mostly happens around you, not inside you. You know enough to nod at the right moments and maybe ask a safe question, but not enough for your pulse to change when somebody starts yelling about legacy, officiating, or lottery odds.
archetypes.quote: Wait, tip-off already? I thought we were just hanging out.
archetypes.teamArchetype: The two-way contract guy who gets waived in training camp.
archetypes.triggerCondition: Triggered when the top score is too weak to claim a real type, when the top two are too close with too little signature evidence, or when the quiz is left blank.

contrast.FAKE: The Finals-only fan at least wakes up for big moments. You do not even do that.
contrast.OJBK: OJBK can at least hang in any hoops conversation. You cannot fake that much range.

archetypes.name: The Six-Pack Spectator
archetypes.shortDesc: For you, the game is half basketball and half drinking event with a scoreboard attached.
archetypes.fullDesc: You treat basketball like a social function that just happens to involve rebounds and defensive breakdowns. Tip-off is the signal to crack something open, the second quarter is when your volume gets irresponsible, and by the fourth you are fully arguing with the television like it insulted your family.
archetypes.quote: Do not tip it yet. I need to open one more.
archetypes.teamArchetype: Milwaukee Bucks. Milwaukee = Beer City. Destiny.
archetypes.triggerCondition: Requires Q17 = D, Heat/F at band 2 or above, and no other archetype reaching 12 or more signature points.

contrast.HYPE: The vibes captain is hyped for the game. You are hyped for the drinking.
contrast.FIRE: The fire-everyone guy is yelling because he cares. You are yelling and forgetting why by tomorrow.

archetypes.name: The GOAT Arbiter
archetypes.shortDesc: Every basketball conversation within range eventually becomes your latest all-time ranking.
archetypes.fullDesc: Nobody gets to say something nice about a player around you without accidentally reopening the all-time list. One compliment becomes peak-versus-longevity, then rings, then supporting cast, then suddenly the room is trapped inside your tenth revision of the top ten.
archetypes.quote: Give me ten seconds. I need to rework the top ten first.
archetypes.playerArchetype: Depends on the day you ask — Monday it's Jordan, Wednesday LeBron, Friday Kobe, weekends TBD.

contrast.STAT: The stats guy brings spreadsheets. You bring rankings.
contrast.MVP: The stat-padding cop questions the numbers. You skip straight to legacy.

archetypes.name: The Brick Prophet
archetypes.shortDesc: You talk with prophet confidence and jinx-with-receipts timing.
archetypes.fullDesc: You call games early, loudly, and with complete emotional commitment. "We are winning by 20." "Series over." "Book the parade." Then the game flips, your old messages get screenshotted, and now you are the evening's entertainment.
archetypes.quote: Lock it in. Actually hold on, let me delete that.
archetypes.playerArchetype: A supercut of iron. 2018 WCF Game 7 Rockets, 0-for-27 from three. Yeah, that one.

contrast.CTRL: The oracle actually calls things. You call them and get clipped for it later.
contrast.HYPE: The vibes captain hypes things up for fun. You genuinely believe your own jinxes.

archetypes.name: The Process Truther
archetypes.shortDesc: A loss feels bad for other people. For you, it can feel productive.
archetypes.fullDesc: A normal fan sees a loss and thinks, this stinks. You see a loss and start doing draft-position math. One useless March win can annoy you for days. A ten-game skid can feel like the franchise finally making responsible choices.
archetypes.quote: Do not win tonight. Think long-term.
archetypes.teamArchetype: 2014-2016 Philadelphia 76ers — the patron saints of tanking.

contrast.DEAD: The ride-or-die fan sees a loss as a loss. You turn it into draft equity.
contrast.MONK: The zen fan stays calm all the time. You only find peace when the team loses correctly.

archetypes.name: The Armchair Coach
archetypes.shortDesc: You have fired a thousand coaches from the couch and still think you could do it better.
archetypes.fullDesc: Timeouts belong to you in spirit. Rotations belong to you in principle. Every late-game possession becomes a fresh opportunity to point at the screen and explain exactly what should have happened two passes ago.
archetypes.quote: Put me in. Not at point guard. On the sideline.
archetypes.playerArchetype: You see Pop in the mirror. The league sees a mid-season interim getting fired by All-Star break.

contrast.FIRE: The fire-everyone guy focuses on who should be gone. You focus on what should have been run.
contrast.STAT: The analytics guy throws numbers at the problem. You throw sets and rotations at it.

archetypes.name: The Analytics Enforcer
archetypes.shortDesc: You do not trust vibes when a spreadsheet exists.
archetypes.fullDesc: While everybody else is talking about killer instinct, confidence, and "you just had to watch it," you are already opening tabs. TS%, on/off, matchup data, shot profile, opponent quality: if the conversation cannot survive contact with numbers, you do not think much of the conversation.
archetypes.quote: Do not tell me how it felt. Show me the numbers.
archetypes.playerArchetype: Your spirit animal isn't a player — it's Daryl Morey.

contrast.COAH: The couch coach wants to talk scheme. You want to talk numbers.
contrast.MVP: The stat-padding cop distrusts the data. You trust it more than people.

archetypes.name: Mr. 58-Point Benchmark
archetypes.shortDesc: Every ugly score has to pass through your private 58-point conversion table.
archetypes.fullDesc: In your head, 58 is not just a score anymore. It is a measuring device. Every blowout gets dragged back to Nuggets 121, Hornets 63 on April 27, 2009 like that box score is the official unit of basketball humiliation.
archetypes.quote: Only lost by 50? That is still just 0.86 of a 58.
archetypes.playerArchetype: You are the 2009 Denver Nuggets — always on the side doing the curb-stomping, never the side on the floor.

contrast.POLO: POLO treats the bit like annual trauma. You treat it like a measurement system.
contrast.MEDI: The McGrady type is romantic about miracles. You are doing scoreboard accounting.

archetypes.name: The Bandwagon Ace
archetypes.shortDesc: Your fandom history looks suspiciously similar to a title timeline.
archetypes.fullDesc: Your team has a funny habit of being whichever team currently looks smartest, deepest, or most likely to be covered in confetti two months from now. New wallpaper, new jersey, new explanation for why this contender just happens to fit your values perfectly.
archetypes.quote: I am not chasing rings. I am appreciating excellence.
archetypes.playerArchetype: You are KD's spiritual descendant — he jumped to four teams, you jumped to fourteen.

contrast.FAKE: The Finals-only fan barely watches. You watch plenty, just always from the winner side.
contrast.OJBK: OJBK truly does not care who wins. You care a lot. You just prefer to be right.

archetypes.name: The Kiss of Death
archetypes.shortDesc: The moment you declare something safe, the universe starts scripting disaster.
archetypes.fullDesc: You are not trying to doom your team. That would almost be easier to explain. The problem is that every time you tweet "we are good" or post something prematurely confident, reality immediately starts looking for a way to embarrass you.
archetypes.quote: I did not jinx it. I just happened to be there when it broke.
archetypes.playerArchetype: J.R. Smith running out the clock in a tied NBA Finals game. Forever.

contrast.58: The 58 guy turns pain into math. You turn it into an anniversary.
contrast.MEDI: The McGrady type keeps miracle memories alive. Your favorite replay is a disaster clip.

archetypes.name: The Hater in Chief
archetypes.shortDesc: Your emotional well-being is heavily indexed to one specific rival failing.
archetypes.fullDesc: You do not always need your own team to win in order to feel joy. Sometimes it is enough for the team you hate to lose. Sometimes, if we are being honest, it is even better.
archetypes.quote: Sure, they won. Still a fraudulent path though.
archetypes.playerArchetype: The star you hate most (you know his splits better than his real fans).

contrast.REF: The ref truther blames the whistle. You just cannot stand other people being happy.
contrast.FIRE: The fire-everyone guy unloads on his own team. You save most of your venom for somebody else.

archetypes.name: The Ref Truther
archetypes.shortDesc: A bad whistle is never random to you. It is evidence.
archetypes.fullDesc: To you, a missed call is never just one bad call. It belongs in a chain. There is always a previous example, a suspicious pattern, a too-convenient outcome, and a replay angle that mysteriously makes everything look worse.
archetypes.quote: Wait, run it back at quarter speed. There it is.
archetypes.playerArchetype: You are the self-appointed chair of the Citizens' Officiating Review Board.

contrast.SALT: The hater-in-chief is jealous of the other side. You are laser-focused on the whistle.
contrast.FIRE: The fire-everyone guy blames the coach. You blame the officials.

archetypes.name: The Fire-Everyone Guy
archetypes.shortDesc: When things go bad, your first instinct is to start firing people in batches.
archetypes.fullDesc: For you, every basketball problem belongs to the same solution family: fire the coach, trade the soft guys, dump the front office, sell the team, start over. Nuance slows the process down. You prefer names, blame, and immediate action.
archetypes.quote: Three straight losses and everybody still has a job?
archetypes.playerArchetype: You are the unofficial GM. None of your trade machines clear the cap.

contrast.COAH: The couch coach wants to talk basketball. You want to talk firings.
contrast.REF: The ref truther blames the whistle. You blame the people getting paid to run the team.

archetypes.name: The Highlight Disciple
archetypes.shortDesc: If nobody got put on a poster, part of you thinks the game was spiritually incomplete.
archetypes.fullDesc: You can respect shooting and spacing in theory, but what you really came for is violence at the rim. One clean poster does more for your soul than six possessions of beautiful half-court execution.
archetypes.quote: That was nice, but did anybody get baptized?
archetypes.playerArchetype: Vince Carter, Zach LaVine, Aaron Gordon — eternal dunk contest gods.

contrast.3PTR: The logo-shot believer chases deep-ball beauty. You want violence at the rim.
contrast.HYPE: The vibes captain gets loud for the post. You get loud for the dunk itself.

archetypes.name: The Logo-Shot Evangelist
archetypes.shortDesc: Deep threes are not all equal in your mind. Steph has his own category.
archetypes.fullDesc: You do not just like three-point shooting. You believe in it, and more specifically, you believe in a very particular, very holy form of it that begins with Steph and, in your opinion, mostly ends there too.
archetypes.quote: When he takes it from there, that is not a bad shot. That is doctrine.
archetypes.playerArchetype: Steph Curry. End of list.

contrast.DUNK: The dunk disciple wants bodies on posters. You want the ball hanging forever in the air.
contrast.MEDI: The McGrady believer chases miracles. You chase shot-making religion.

archetypes.name: The Buzzer-Beater Believer
archetypes.shortDesc: You never fully believe a game is over because miracles have already rewired your brain.
archetypes.fullDesc: Down twelve late is not a death sentence to you. It is an invitation to remember every insane comeback clip that ever lodged itself in your brain and convinced you that no broadcast is truly safe to leave early.
archetypes.quote: Do not turn it off. Weird things happen fast.
archetypes.playerArchetype: T-Mac (35 seconds, 13 points) + Reggie Miller (8 points, 8.9 seconds) + every game-winner ever cut.

contrast.3PTR: The logo-shot believer worships range. You worship ridiculous endings.
contrast.POLO: POLO keeps reliving trauma. You keep reliving fairy tales.

archetypes.name: The Ride-or-Die Martyr
archetypes.shortDesc: If the team goes down, you go down with them. That is the contract.
archetypes.fullDesc: You are not switching teams. Not after the bad years, not during the bad years, not if the bad years somehow become their own geological period. You can complain about the roster, drag the coach, roast ownership, and still refuse to walk away.
archetypes.quote: I am not leaving. Somebody has to stay and suffer correctly.
archetypes.teamArchetype: Die-hards for the Kings, Hornets, Pistons, or any perennially-cooked franchise.

contrast.TANK: The tanker can smile through a loss. You just endure it.
contrast.MONK: The zen fan genuinely stays detached. You are attached all the way down.

archetypes.name: The Lone Wolf Spectator
archetypes.shortDesc: You like your basketball quiet, private, and free of extra commentary.
archetypes.fullDesc: You do not hate people. You just think most of them make watching basketball worse. Watch parties talk through possessions. Group chats flatten moments before you can think about them. Somebody always needs something explained.
archetypes.quote: I do not need company to know what I just watched.
archetypes.playerArchetype: Mamba mentality, west-coast edition — alone in the gym at 4am (you're alone watching 4am tip-offs on League Pass).

contrast.HYPE: The vibes captain needs the post to feel complete. You do not need witnesses.
contrast.MONK: The zen fan drifts above it all. You actively choose to watch alone.

archetypes.name: The Stat-Padding Auditor
archetypes.shortDesc: Every huge scoring night gets an immediate asterisk check from you.
archetypes.fullDesc: Big counting stats do not move you on their own. Somebody drops 40 and the room starts celebrating. You are already checking pace, usage, shot diet, opponent quality, and how much of that damage happened after the game had already gone soft.
archetypes.quote: Forty is cute. Open the garbage-time filter.
archetypes.playerArchetype: Westbrook's averaged-triple-double season — the original padding vs. production flame war.

contrast.STAT: The analytics guy trusts the numbers. You interrogate them.
contrast.GOAT: The GOAT debater skips straight to legacy. You stop first to ask how fake the stat line was.

archetypes.name: The 'Called It' Oracle
archetypes.shortDesc: You love being right early almost as much as you love the game itself.
archetypes.fullDesc: Predictions are not little throwaway comments to you. They are investments. If one hits, you are not moving on until everyone in the chat has acknowledged that you called it before the rest of the herd caught on.
archetypes.quote: Scroll up. I said this was coming.
archetypes.playerArchetype: Paul the Octopus, but for basketball.

contrast.STAT: The analytics guy wins arguments with numbers. You win them with receipts from the past.
contrast.IRON: The brick prophet gets caught being wrong. You get to say you told them so.

archetypes.name: The Finals-Only Fan
archetypes.shortDesc: You reappear when the lights get bright and disappear when the schedule gets ordinary.
archetypes.fullDesc: You are not around all year, and you are not pretending otherwise. Finals season, Christmas, big trades, legacy arguments, maybe one or two marquee games if the timeline gets loud enough. The rest of the calendar barely knows you.
archetypes.quote: Wait, is number 23 LeBron or Jordan in this context?
archetypes.playerArchetype: You're the target audience for every Drake Instagram Story. Champagne Papi, champagne podium.

contrast.BAND: The bandwagon fan actually stays tuned in. You mainly reappear when the lights are brightest.
contrast.BNCH: The bench-seat type cannot even catch the references. You at least know the big ones.

archetypes.name: The Vibes Captain
archetypes.shortDesc: The basketball matters, but the chat reaction, screenshots, and energy matter almost as much.
archetypes.fullDesc: For you, the game is only half the event. The other half is the all-caps reaction, the first screenshot, the perfectly timed post, the little social explosion that proves everybody else felt it too.
archetypes.quote: Huge shot. Bigger group-chat moment.
archetypes.playerArchetype: You're the NBA's hype man — no flip, plenty of shout.

contrast.SOLO: The lone watcher does not need anyone else there. You do.
contrast.ATM: The spender proves love with receipts. You prove it with volume and screenshots.

archetypes.name: The Zen Spectator
archetypes.shortDesc: You care about basketball without letting it hijack your nervous system.
archetypes.fullDesc: Bad loss? Tough. Great win? Nice. Wild finish? Interesting. You are engaged, but never fully captured, which makes you look alien inside fan spaces built on overreaction.
archetypes.quote: If I catch it, great. If not, life continues.
archetypes.teamArchetype: Sacramento Kings fans pre-2023 — 16 straight years out of the playoffs teaches you things.

contrast.DEAD: The ride-or-die fan keeps suffering on purpose. You are too detached for that.
contrast.OJBK: OJBK really has no strong preference. You are calm because you refuse to over-invest.

archetypes.name: The League Pass ATM
archetypes.shortDesc: Your fandom absolutely has a transaction history.
archetypes.fullDesc: League Pass, jerseys, sneakers, tickets, random collabs, collector nonsense you definitely did not need but bought anyway — your love of basketball leaves a paper trail.
archetypes.quote: This is not overspending. This is supporting the cause.
archetypes.playerArchetype: The NBA's favorite kind of fan: terminal consumer.

contrast.HYPE: The vibes captain posts through the love. You pay through it.
contrast.HOOP: The pickup hero proves love by playing. You prove it by buying.

archetypes.name: The Pickup Legend (Self-Declared)
archetypes.shortDesc: Your confidence arrives at the court well before your actual jumper does.
archetypes.fullDesc: You believe in your game with a level of commitment the evidence does not always reward. Misses are contextual. Rim was off. Legs were dead. Sun was weird. The shot itself was fine.
archetypes.quote: I can make that. The conditions are just weird today.
archetypes.playerArchetype: The gap between your pickup self-image and your pickup game is bigger than the gap between Draymond's jumper and his confidence in it.

contrast.COAH: The couch coach wants to diagram it. You want to get on the floor and do it.
contrast.DUNK: The dunk disciple is excited to watch somebody fly. You still think you might be that somebody.

archetypes.name: The Team Mom
archetypes.shortDesc: You do not just root for players. You worry about them like they are your own overworked kids.
archetypes.fullDesc: Minutes load, injury history, recovery timelines, coaching decisions, media pressure — all of it lands in your brain as a form of concern. You can enjoy the game, sure, but your first instinct is still protective.
archetypes.quote: Please sit him for a week. I am asking nicely.
archetypes.playerArchetype: Every rookie's unofficial internet mom.

contrast.DEAD: The ride-or-die fan mainly cares whether the team wins. You are checking whether the kid should sit.
contrast.MONK: The zen fan stays cool about everything. Injuries are where you finally lose that calm.

archetypes.name: Basketball Switzerland
archetypes.shortDesc: You are weirdly okay with almost everything, which makes you suspiciously healthy.
archetypes.fullDesc: Strong hatred does not really live in you. Favorite team, sure. Favorite player, fine. But the idea of letting sports colonize your entire emotional system never fully took.
archetypes.quote: Whoever. Whatever. I can watch that.
archetypes.playerArchetype: You are the undrafted free agent — not bad, just nothing screamed "take him."
archetypes.note: Gated archetype (only appears when none of the five axes goes extreme)
archetypes.triggerCondition: Requires all five dimensions to stay out of band 3, with OJBK still ranking first on its own signature plus base score.

contrast.BNCH: The bench-seat type cannot keep up with the conversation. You can float through any of them.
contrast.MONK: The zen fan made an active choice to rise above it. You just never fell all the way in.
